name: Lior Samfiru
notes: One of the most recognized employment law firms in Ontario. Strong media presence and track record with severance negotiations for professionals earning $100K+. Offices in Toronto, Ottawa, and Calgary.

name: Andrew Monkhouse
notes: Boutique employment law firm focused exclusively on employee-side representation. Known for handling complex severance cases involving stock options and executive compensation.

name: Alex Lucifero
notes: Ottawa managing partner. Specializes in federal government employee terminations and public sector severance. Regular CTV Ottawa contributor.

name: Daniel Lublin
notes: One of Toronto's most prominent employment lawyers. Regularly quoted in Globe and Mail and National Post. Focuses on executive-level terminations and complex non-compete disputes.

name: Stacey Ball
notes: Author of 'Canadian Employment Law'. Deep expertise in constructive dismissal cases and workplace toxicity situations. Often handles high-profile cases.

name: Arash Babaei
notes: Employee-side firm with a focus on discrimination, accommodation failures, and severance for mid-career professionals. Responsive and transparent about fees.

name: Howard Levitt
notes: Canada's most recognized employment lawyer. Nationally syndicated columnist. Represents executives and senior professionals. Fees reflect his reputation but his expertise is unmatched.

name: Alex Achkar
notes: Firm handles both employer and employee side but has strong employee-side severance review services. Good educational content on their site. Multiple Ontario locations.

name: Muneeza Sheikh
notes: Partner at Levitt Sheikh. Strong focus on human rights, workplace harassment, and discrimination cases alongside standard severance work. Frequent media commentator.

name: Stephen Moreau
notes: Established labour and employment firm. Good option for unionized workers or situations involving class-action layoffs. Also handles human rights tribunal cases.

name: Nikolay Y. Chsherbinin
notes: Hamilton-based firm with strong severance negotiation practice. Serves the Hamilton, Burlington, and Niagara region. Free initial consultation.

name: Michael Smyth
notes: Hamilton and Burlington employment law. Employee-side focus. Accessible and straightforward about what you can expect from a severance review.

name: Doug MacLeod
notes: London, Ontario employment lawyer. Employee-side only. Known for the 'Ask An Employment Lawyer' blog. Clear, no-nonsense approach to severance reviews.

name: George Dube
notes: Kitchener-Waterloo region. Handles a lot of tech sector layoffs from the KW corridor. Familiar with RSU and stock option issues common in tech severance packages.

name: Randy Ai
notes: Employee-side firm with offices in Toronto and Markham. Strong online presence with educational content. Handles severance reviews for mid-level professionals.

name: Lorenzo Lisi
notes: Senior partner at a major Bay Street firm. Best suited for executive-level terminations with complex equity, non-compete, or multi-jurisdictional issues. Higher fee tier but appropriate for high-value packages.

name: Stuart Rudner
notes: Author of 'You're Chicken Soup for the Wrongfully Dismissed Soul'. Media commentator. Also does employment mediation. Based in Markham, serves Greater Toronto Area.

name: David Bleakney
notes: One of BC's largest employment and labour law firms. Handles both sides but has experienced employee-side practitioners. Good for complex BC employment matters.

name: Erin Brandt
notes: Employee-side boutique firm in Greater Vancouver. Modern approach, transparent pricing. Good first call for BC professionals who want a clear assessment without hard-sell tactics.

name: Nadia Zaman
notes: Offices in Vancouver, Calgary, and Edmonton. Employee-side focus. Good option if your layoff involves cross-provincial issues between BC and Alberta.

name: Bow River Law
notes: Calgary-based employment law firm. Experienced with oil and gas sector layoffs, which have specific nuances around bonus structures and field work arrangements.

name: Charles Frenette
notes: Calgary office of Taylor Janis. Employee-focused employment lawyers. Strong presence in Alberta market. Free consultations.

name: Taylor Janis LLP (Edmonton)
notes: Edmonton office. Same employee-side focus as the Calgary and Vancouver locations. Good option for Northern Alberta professionals.

name: Catherine McKenzie
notes: English and French. Montreal firm with strong employment law practice. Quebec has different labour standards (CNESST), so it's important to use a lawyer who knows the provincial system.

name: Law Society Referral Services
notes: Every province has a Law Society referral service that will connect you with an employment lawyer for a free 30-minute consultation. This is the best starting point if you don't know where to begin. Ontario: LSO, BC: Access Pro Bono, Alberta: Calgary Legal Guidance / Edmonton Community Legal Centre.
